beat: A junior partner at a no-name fund slides into your DMs after seeing your post; the 'meeting' is a 20-minute coffee at Blue Bottle and they keep checking their phone.
Peter Thiel slides a contrarian term sheet across the bar at Rosewood Sand Hill: no board seat, conditional on you saying something publicly heretical about your industry.
Marc Andreessen pulls you aside at an a16z dinner and quotes his own essay at you, then sketches a $20M Series A on a napkin if you reframe the company as 'AI-native' on the spot.
The associate who pursued you last week emails: the partnership passed. The kicker is the 'feedback' — copy-pasted from your own pitch deck. You can call them out or write a polite reply.
A VC you didn't ask for cold-emails you anyway, framing 'we love bootstrappers' until the call where they pitch you on raising. They mention three competitors who took their money.
A founder you barely know corners you at a YC dinner and pitches themselves as your missing cofounder, complete with a slide deck about you they made on the BART ride over.
Your cofounder calls a 1:1 at the office on a Sunday. They've been talking to a recruiter. They want to know if you'd 'still be friends' if they took the offer.
Your cofounder forwards you a Discord screenshot. It's you, six weeks ago, planning to dilute their equity. They want an answer before the 4pm board call.

7am, your kitchen, half a mug of cold brew. The email from an old YC batchmate has been open on your laptop for an hour — they want to be your cofounder. You re-read it three times looking for the catch. The room is very quiet.
A staff writer at a mid-tier newsletter wants 'just 15 minutes for a backgrounder.' Their first question — 'so, no real moat, right?' — lands like a knife through butter.
A reporter has receipts: three Slack screenshots from someone you fired, your old pitch deck claiming numbers you didn't have, and a quote from an investor you ghosted. Story drops Friday.
A glossy magazine wants to put you on the cover. The interviewer is reverent. Their angle: 'the next Stripe.' You can lean in or correct the record.
A second-tier Twitter account quote-tweets your launch with three crying-laughing emojis. The thread is now 200 replies deep. Two of them are from people you respect.
Sahil Lavingia sub-tweets you specifically: 'another bootstrapper who'll fold by Q4.' His thread already has 800 likes. Your DMs are a graveyard of 'you good?' messages.
A second-time founder offers to grab coffee. They spend twelve minutes telling you about their company, three minutes asking about yours, and end with 'happy to help, just ping me.'
Paul Graham emails you a four-line response. It's a link to one of his old essays and the words 'this one.' The essay is twelve years old and exactly about what you're doing wrong.
Sam Altman replies to your DM in 90 seconds. 'Interesting. Send me a deck.' That's it. Three words, three hours, and your inbox lights up with seventeen new investor cold-emails.
A mentor you trust takes you to the back of Tartine and tells you, calmly, that you're becoming someone they wouldn't introduce to their friends. They mean it kindly. It still hits.

3am. Dolores Park is empty except for raccoons and the city humming below. You're on the cracked bench by the J-line tracks, laptop open to a deck you haven't touched in two hours, asking yourself the question you've been postponing.
Caltrain to Mountain View, 4:35pm, half-empty car. The hills past Burlingame roll by. Your phone hasn't pinged in 47 minutes and you realize you can't remember the last time it was that quiet.
Sunday, 11am. You meant to write code. Instead you've refreshed Twitter for forty minutes — there's a thread about your space and you're 30 quote-tweets deep on someone you don't know.
4:13am. Sentry alert. The scaling bug from beta is back, hitting paying customers now. The office is empty. Your coffee went cold three hours ago. Your laptop is the only light on the floor.
Tuesday morning. Product Hunt #1 is a startup that does what you do — but with an AI agent layer you weren't planning. Their landing page reads like someone with $4M in the bank wrote it.
X locks your account at 2:14pm. The notice is one paragraph and three appeal links. Yesterday's 12k-impression mentions tab is a 404. The DMs you needed for tomorrow's call are gone.
You wake to 14,318 unread notifications. The one-line tweet from 11pm — the one ending in 'unhinged take' — is at 2.3M impressions. Three reporters and two recruiters have DMed 'are you free?'
9:14pm Friday. The W26 application closes at 11:59. You haven't submitted. You opened the form last week and saved a draft that says 'we're building…' and trailed off.
7am, Ocean Beach, fog so thick the Pacific is a sound not a view. You walk it end to end. Somewhere around the second mile the decision you've been postponing for three weeks makes itself.
First doctor's visit since college. Bloodwork is fine. The doctor asks, very gently, when you last took a day off. You can't answer the question. The fluorescent light is too bright.
Sunday 2:14am. Stripe dashboard pings. First paying customer — $9, from someone in Berlin. You screenshot it twice. You don't sleep again for the rest of the night.
11:47pm Tuesday. The runway spreadsheet on your second monitor. Burn rate, MRR, three scenarios. You re-do the math four times trying to get a different answer. The answer is the same answer.

The walk from Sand Hill back to the Caltrain. Fifteen minutes through eucalyptus. You replay the meeting in your head and notice three answers you should have given. Your reflection in the train window doesn't look like you remember.

2:34pm Tuesday. OpenAI's keynote ends. They just shipped 80% of what you do, free, in their dev tools. Your team Slack is silent for nine minutes. The first message is from the most junior engineer: 'so… do we ship anyway?'

Tuesday morning. A friend's company — started six months after yours, in an adjacent space — just announced an $80M acquisition. Their tweet is at 12k likes. Your phone buzzes with three texts asking if you've seen it.

Wednesday 10am. YC announces the W26 batch. You scan the company list. Two of them are doing exactly what you proposed in your application six months ago. Their listed founders are people you've never heard of.
Stripe email at 4:11pm: 'we're holding your transactions for review.' No phone number. The dashboard shows $47k frozen. Customer support reply ETA: 2-3 business days. Payroll runs Friday.
